metrics.narrative: UCL quantified the marketplace opportunity during discovery on 2026-03-09: approximately £40 million annual marketplace spend (over half with STEM suppliers), of which more than 90% currently bypasses the portal. Nnenna stated the goal is to route roughly 50% of spend under £75k through a compliant marketplace with quote trails and procurement guardrails, which would represent meaningful adoption improvement. The business problem is fragmented data access, poor data quality (inconsistent UNSPSC taxonomy, missing MPNs, irrelevant product images), and lack of AI-driven analytics or guided buying, all of which hinder spend visibility, compliance, and user adoption. Pain points include manual SQL exports for reporting, offline contract approvals via DocuSign with no AI risk review, and a legacy marketplace with no supplier catalog governance. However, UCL has NOT quantified the cost of these inefficiencies in financial terms (FTE burden, savings leakage, compliance risk exposure) — the value case remains qualitative. The compelling event is the One Advanced contract expiring early 2028, but the immediate forcing function is internal budget approval, which is the current blocker.

champion.narrative: Nnenna Isiadinso (Project Manager, data analytics and STEM category lead) is the current champion — she is knowledgeable, technically sophisticated, and has driven all engagement with Zycus (discovery call 2026-03-09, demos 2026-03-19 and 2026-04-13, use-case scoping). She clearly articulated UCL's pain, requirements, and decision timeline, and she invited two directors of supply chain and two SMEs to the demo, which signals her ability to convene senior stakeholders. However, her championship is developing and at risk: (1) She is an evaluator and influencer (Project Manager, not a director or budget holder), so her access to the Economic Buyer is unconfirmed — she mentioned 'my boss' but did not name them or bring them into the conversation. (2) She has not advocated publicly for Zycus over competitors (Jaggaer, Coupa) — she is running a fair RFP evaluation, not pushing Zycus internally. (3) Most critically, she has paused vendor engagement and explicitly told Zycus to wait while UCL builds the internal business case, which signals either that her advocacy has not yet secured budget approval or that she lacks the authority to drive that decision. Her engagement was active and two-way in March-April 2026 (three touchpoints in 5 weeks), but has been silent since mid-April (74 days as of 2026-06-26), and her last message was a hold signal, not a pull-forward. The champion is real and capable, but currently passive — she is waiting on internal approvals, not driving urgency. Zycus must re-engage her, understand the internal blockers, and help her build the business case to secure budget, or escalate to her boss (the EB) directly if she cannot move the decision forward.

competition.narrative: UCL is evaluating multiple vendors as part of their source-to-contract and procure-to-pay RFP process. The competitive field, as named on the 2026-03-09 discovery call: (1) One Advanced (incumbent) — provides current sourcing and contract management, contract expires early 2028, described by Nnenna as 'doing enough' but lacking desired capabilities (no AI, limited workflow automation, repository-only contract system). Status: incumbent being replaced, low current threat as they are not defending aggressively, but their contract term gives UCL no urgency until late 2027. (2) Jaggaer and Coupa — explicitly mentioned by Anthony (Zycus VP) as competitors 'in the wheelhouse' that UCL is likely considering; Nnenna did not object or correct, which implies they are in the evaluation set. Both are Tier-1 S2P platforms with strong UK public-sector presence. Status: active, threat level medium to high (peer competitors, not yet shortlisted but likely in the RFP). (3) Tend — current tendering platform, with an eQuotes module Nnenna said is 'not fit for purpose,' so UCL cannot use it. Status: partial incumbent, likely to be replaced, low threat (already failing on use case). (4) G-Cloud framework — Nnenna mentioned 'we've looked at G-Cloud' as a procurement route, which may constrain vendor choice to G-Cloud-listed platforms. This is a process competitor (a buying channel), not a product competitor. The strongest current threat is the status quo: UCL's budget approval is incomplete, and if they cannot secure funding, the incumbent One Advanced will remain in place by default until 2028. The RFP has not launched, so no vendor has been shortlisted or scored. Zycus's wedge is AI-driven data governance, guided buying, and spend analytics — capabilities One Advanced and Tend lack, and areas where Zycus can differentiate vs Jaggaer and Coupa if the demos and business case land. However, Zycus has not yet positioned this wedge in a competitive review or directly against named rivals, and Nnenna has paused engagement, so the competitive battle has not been joined.

identify_pain.narrative: UCL's pain is operational inefficiency and data fragmentation across their procure-to-pay and source-to-contract landscape, owned by Nnenna (data analytics and STEM category project lead) and the broader procurement team of approximately 40 people. Specific pains articulated on the 2026-03-09 discovery call: (1) Fragmented data access requiring manual SQL exports and manipulation because the legacy marketplace system has no self-serve BI ('my data team is quite advanced, so we're doing a lot with the data. But we're having to export import into SQL'); (2) Poor data quality — inconsistent UNSPSC codes (suppliers omit them, system assigns high-level defaults), mismatched MPNs and brand names, and product images that are supplier logos rather than actual product photos, all of which break guided buying accuracy ('if your data is not even comparable, it's not really the intelligent guiding experience'); (3) Low marketplace adoption — over 90% of the £40M addressable spend bypasses the portal because the system is complex, supplier fees deter participation, and search results are often irrelevant, requiring manual curation; (4) Offline contract approvals via DocuSign with no AI for risk analysis or redline review ('we're using the system for is literally a repository'); (5) Lack of spend visibility and supplier performance analytics — procurement cannot enforce compliance or demonstrate savings without labor-intensive custom reporting. The pain owner is the procurement and commercial leadership (including the two directors of supply chain), with Nnenna as the technical pain champion and voice of requirements. The pain is real and quantified (90% leakage, £40M addressable), but UCL has not yet secured budget to solve it, which is the current blocker.

UCL's internal sequence is: (1) secure budget approval to proceed (decision deadline was June 2026 per Nnenna on 2026-03-09); (2) draft and issue RFP for source-to-contract and procure-to-pay (best-case timing end of 2026 per Nnenna's latest update); (3) evaluate vendors (Nnenna confirmed they are exploring Jaggaer, Coupa, and others, and considering G-Cloud procurement framework); (4) select and implement by early 2028 to replace expiring One Advanced contract. decision_criteria.narrative: UCL's decision criteria emerged clearly from the discovery and use-case scoping on 2026-03-09. The core requirements are: (1) robust data governance with standardized UNSPSC taxonomy at the granularity UCL requires, and agility to customize categorization (Nnenna emphasized 'UNSPSC is an international standard... everybody knows what UNSPSC is' and insisted on control over catalog templates and data input standards); (2) AI-driven guided buying that accurately recommends identical items from preferred suppliers with cost-saving prompts, dependent on high-quality product data (MPNs, brand names, accurate images); (3) self-serve spend analytics and BI dashboards eliminating manual SQL exports; (4) streamlined supplier onboarding and marketplace adoption (low complexity, no supplier fees that deter participation); (5) AI-powered contract review for risk analysis and redline management (currently done offline); (6) seamless Oracle ERP integration (Oracle is staying, unlikely to be replaced, may be upgraded). Nnenna stated she has 'very strong opinions' on data quality and catalog acceptability, and that any vendor recommendation must align with UCL's specific needs, not just best-practice templates. The RFP will evaluate source-to-contract and procure-to-pay modules together. Zycus shared an RFP primer and delivered two demos (2026-03-19 and 2026-04-13, both not recorded but the 2026-04-27 demo marked successful), so criteria fit has been partially validated, but no formal scoring or shortlist has been shared.

ai_fit_signal.summary: UCL is AI Curious with strong appetite for AI-driven capabilities, but the AIS field is not populated, so there is no scored signal. Nnenna's requirements on the 2026-03-09 discovery call were explicitly AI-forward: she asked about AI for contract review ('Are you using any AI with that current platform in terms of reviewing red lines, looking at the risk of the contract?'), emphasized the need for 'AI-driven spend visibility, supplier stratification, self-serve analytics,' and validated guided buying intelligence dependent on high-quality data as a top priority. Anthony positioned Zycus's autonomous negotiation agent, AI contract capabilities, and spend analytics in response, and the demos included Merlin and ANA per the Products field. However, there is no evidence that UCL has elevated AI as a strategic priority beyond these functional requirements — no C-level AI mandate, no enterprise AI transformation program mentioned. The appetite is real and functional, not strategic or executive-driven, which places UCL in the AI Curious tier: they want AI where it solves their problems (data quality, guided buying, contract risk, analytics), but it is not the headline or the forcing function.

north_star_verdict.headline: UCL explicitly paused Zycus until budget and business case are approved. Buyer requested no further action; best-case RFP timing is late 2026. Zero buyer engagement for 74 days; rep is chasing into silence. Deal is parked pending internal sign-off.

best_practice_check.summary: UCL is stalled on internal budget approval, not vendor selection, and the single-threaded relationship through Nnenna (an evaluator, not a budget holder) leaves Zycus blind to the internal business case and EB decision. With the RFP best-case timing pushed to end of 2026 (6+ months out) and no buyer engagement for 74 days, the deal is at risk of indefinite delay or competitor pre-emption. The highest-leverage actions are: (1) help Nnenna quantify the cost of inaction (manual FTE burden, 90% spend leakage, data-quality risk) and the ROI of Zycus to arm her internal advocacy; (2) multi-thread to the two directors of supply chain who attended the demo and the Economic Buyer (likely one of the directors or the executive sponsor) to understand and influence the budget decision; (3) position Zycus's AI and data-governance differentiation vs Jaggaer and Coupa before the RFP launches, so UCL sees Zycus as the specialist choice, not one of several options.

competitive_position.summary: UCL is running a multi-vendor evaluation for source-to-contract and procure-to-pay, but the RFP has not yet launched (awaiting internal budget approval). The incumbent One Advanced is being replaced (contract expires early 2028) but poses low active threat — they are a legacy provider doing 'enough' with no AI or modern workflow capabilities. The live peer threats are Jaggaer and Coupa, both Tier-1 S2P platforms with strong UK public-sector presence, mentioned as evaluation targets on the 2026-03-09 discovery call; status is active but not yet shortlisted (RFP pending). Tend (current tendering platform) is a weak partial incumbent with a 'not fit for purpose' eQuotes module, likely to be replaced, low threat. G-Cloud procurement framework was mentioned as a potential sourcing route, which may constrain vendor choice to G-Cloud-listed platforms. The single strongest current threat is the status quo and budget inertia: UCL has paused vendor engagement while building the internal business case, and if budget approval fails, One Advanced remains in place by default until 2028. Zycus has delivered three demos (latest 2026-04-27 marked successful) and shared an RFP primer, positioning on AI-driven data governance, guided buying, and spend analytics, but no competitive review or head-to-head differentiation vs Jaggaer/Coupa has occurred. The competitive battle is latent, not active — it will begin when the RFP launches (best-case end of 2026 per Nnenna).

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is under-developed. UCL's requirements are AI-heavy — guided buying intelligence, self-serve spend analytics, contract risk and redline AI, data-quality governance — and Nnenna explicitly asked about AI capabilities on the 2026-03-09 discovery call ('Are you using any AI with that current platform in terms of reviewing red lines, looking at the risk of the contract?'). Anthony positioned Zycus's AI capabilities in the discovery call (autonomous negotiation agent, AI-driven spend visibility, contract AI), and the demos included Merlin and ANA per the Products field, but there is no evidence that the AI story landed as a differentiator or was validated in the demos. The 2026-04-27 demo was marked 'successful,' but no use-case scoring, AI proof points, or buyer feedback on the AI capabilities has been captured. With Jaggaer and Coupa in the competitive set, Zycus's AI depth (Merlin negotiation, contract AI, guided-buying intelligence) is the wedge, but it has not been positioned head-to-head vs competitors or tied to UCL's business case (cost of poor data quality, FTE savings from self-serve analytics, compliance improvement from contract AI). AI is a requirement, not yet a differentiated strength.

items.quote: Require robust data governance with standard UNSPSC taxonomy and agile categorization. UNSPSC is an international standard. So for me, everybody knows what UNSPSC is. We need the agility in the platform to adapt to your specific needs.
items.criterion: AI-driven data governance with UNSPSC standardization at required granularity and agility to customize taxonomy

items.quote: Require guided buying to prompt users with identical items and potential cost savings. If your data is not even comparable, it's not really the intelligent guiding experience. Inconsistent catalog details like MPNs or brand names and irrelevant images degrade guided buying accuracy.
items.criterion: Guided buying with accurate recommendations based on high-quality product data (MPNs, brand names, images)

items.quote: Need AI-driven spend visibility, supplier stratification, self-serve analytics for savings, compliance, performance. Data analysis requires manual exports into SQL; self-serve BI not fully available.
items.criterion: Self-serve spend analytics and BI dashboards, eliminating manual SQL exports

items.quote: Contract approvals handled offline; lack of AI for risk analysis and red-line review. Are you using any AI with that current platform in terms of reviewing red lines, looking at the risk of the contract? No.
items.criterion: AI-powered contract review for risk analysis and redline management

items.quote: ERP system is Oracle; upgrade possible but replacement unlikely. You can actually get the agent to negotiate on your behalf... the PO is then drafted, and then sent to a supplier. The invoice is validated, and the payment is then processed.
items.criterion: Seamless Oracle ERP integration (Oracle staying, unlikely to be replaced)

items.quote: At the moment, our suppliers pay to get on the marketplace platform. A lot of yourselves were not charging suppliers to be on the platform and I guess passing back the cost one way or the other to buyers like ourselves. Some of the suppliers we want to get on it are not prepared to because they don't wanna pay.
items.criterion: Low supplier adoption friction (no supplier fees, simple onboarding)

customer_expectations_fit.summary: UCL's expectations are well-documented and Zycus capabilities are a strong fit on paper. The core requirements — AI-driven data governance with UNSPSC standardization and agile categorization, guided buying accuracy dependent on high-quality catalog data, self-serve spend analytics, contract AI for risk review, seamless Oracle integration, and low-friction marketplace adoption — align directly with Zycus's S2P suite (iContract, iSource, eProcurement, Merlin, ANA). Nnenna emphasized data quality as a non-negotiable, and Zycus's catalog governance, taxonomy controls, and AI-driven duplicate detection are purpose-built for this. However, fit is partially validated, not confirmed: three demos were delivered (2026-03-19, 2026-04-13, 2026-04-27, the last marked 'successful'), but no formal use-case validation, scoring, or shortlist feedback has been shared, and the deal is paused awaiting UCL's internal budget approval. The risk is that fit is assumed, not proven, and competitors Jaggaer and Coupa will validate fit in parallel once the RFP launches. Zycus must convert the 'successful demo' into documented proof points (use-case scorecards, reference-customer validation, ROI model) before the RFP, or risk being one of several 'good enough' options.

hard.ais_why:
Single transcript shows interest in intelligent guided buying: 'We want the system to be able to identify and say... this supplier is selling exactly the same thing... this is your saving'—AI-adjacent, not explicit AI demand.
Focus is data access and catalog quality ('our biggest issue is the data access'); no Merlin/agentic enthusiasm expressed.
Three demo meetings were not recorded, limiting AI-signal visibility; budget gate 'by June.' Medium confidence.

value.how_we_win: Help Nnenna build the business case by quantifying the cost of current inefficiencies (FTE burden on manual SQL exports, savings leakage from 90% spend bypass, compliance risk, supplier adoption friction) and the ROI of AI-driven guided buying, data governance, and spend analytics. Offer a TCO model, reference customers in UK higher education, and propose an executive business-case workshop with the deal owner's manager and the procurement directors to secure budget approval.

packets.subject: Status quo / budget inertia

value.name: One Advanced
value.quote: One Advanced provides current sourcing and contract management systems. Contract management by One Advanced serves mainly as a repository for ~900 live contracts and ~600 suppliers. It's doing enough. So we are in a space where we are we imported a bunch of contractors, and we're kinda working through storing documentation. At the moment, they are not connected. We're using the system for is literally a repository.
value.source: Avoma discovery call
value.how_we_win: One Advanced is a legacy repository with no AI, no workflow automation, no guided buying, and no self-serve analytics — exactly the gaps UCL wants to solve. Position Zycus as the modern, AI-native alternative that delivers the intelligent experience One Advanced cannot. Highlight contract AI (risk review, redline analysis), data governance with UNSPSC standardization, and marketplace adoption improvements (low supplier friction, accurate guided buying) as the direct wedge.

packets.subject: One Advanced

value.name: Jaggaer
value.quote: Jaggaer and Coupa mentioned as competing platforms in RFP evaluation.
value.source: Avoma discovery call (Anthony mentioned them, Nnenna did not object)
value.how_we_win: Jaggaer is a strong Tier-1 peer with deep UK public-sector presence and G-Cloud listing. Differentiate on AI: Zycus Merlin autonomous negotiation agent, AI-driven guided buying with savings prompts, and self-serve spend analytics. Emphasize data governance agility (UNSPSC customization, catalog quality controls) as Nnenna's top requirement. Run a competitive review before the RFP launches, positioning Zycus as the AI-first, data-quality-first choice vs Jaggaer's broader but less specialized suite.

packets.subject: Jaggaer

value.name: Coupa
value.quote: Jaggaer and Coupa mentioned as competing platforms in RFP evaluation.
value.source: Avoma discovery call (Anthony mentioned them, Nnenna did not object)
value.how_we_win: Coupa is a Tier-1 cloud S2P leader with strong spend analytics and BSM capabilities, likely the most recognized brand in the field. Differentiate on procurement-AI depth: Zycus Merlin for negotiation, contract AI for risk/redline, and guided buying intelligence tuned to Nnenna's data-quality requirements (UNSPSC granularity, MPN/brand matching, image accuracy). Position Zycus as the specialist vs Coupa's generalist platform, and emphasize UK customer references in higher education or public sector. Run a competitive review before the RFP to anchor the differentiation.

packets.subject: Coupa

value.name: Tend
value.quote: Tend used for tendering; eQuotes module not fit for purpose. We're trying to push our kind of procurement rules a bit more, so 3 quotes for under 5k. It's not fit for purpose, so we can't use it.
value.source: Avoma discovery call
value.how_we_win: Tend is a weak partial incumbent — UCL already declared the eQuotes module unusable. Position Zycus iSource as the fit-for-purpose sourcing and RFx solution that Tend cannot deliver, with AI-driven quote analysis and compliance workflow that UCL needs for tail-spend governance.

packets.subject: Tend

value.name: G-Cloud framework
value.quote: G-Cloud referenced as procurement marketplace considered for platform sourcing.
value.source: Avoma discovery call
value.how_we_win: G-Cloud is a public-sector procurement framework, not a product competitor, but it may constrain vendor choice to G-Cloud-listed platforms. Confirm whether Zycus is G-Cloud listed (if not, this is a blocker); if listed, emphasize the streamlined procurement process and compliance G-Cloud provides. This is a process advantage, not a product wedge.

packets.subject: G-Cloud framework

value.detail: UCL has paused vendor engagement while building the internal business case to secure budget approval, which was due by June 2026 but is now incomplete. Nnenna stated 'we need to decide if we can afford to use the system' and asked 'are we dealing with quarter of 1,000,000, half 1,000,000, 1,000,000?' The business case is not quantified (no ROI model, no cost-of-pain analysis), and the Economic Buyer has not been engaged. If budget approval fails, the deal dies or delays indefinitely, and the incumbent One Advanced remains in place until their contract expires early 2028. This is the single highest-risk vulnerability.

value.detail: The close date 2027-07-30 (399 days out) does not align with the buyer's stated timeline. Nnenna said the budget decision was due by June 2026, and best-case RFP tender timing is end of 2026, with contract replacement needed by early 2028 (One Advanced contract expiry). A best-case RFP launch in Q4 2026 implies vendor selection in Q1-Q2 2027 and implementation through late 2027, which could align with the close date, but the deal is currently stalled in Qualified stage with no RFP launch date confirmed. If the budget decision slips further or the RFP is delayed into 2027, the close date will slip 6-12 months. The forecast is Pipeline, which is appropriate, but the date is optimistic given the current stall.
